topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification pinged, a sharp, insistent sound against the quiet hum of my laptop. I blinked, my thumb hovering over the screen, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My breath hitched. I hadn't realized how much I’d been watching his posts—the curated glimpses into his weekend hikes, the thoughtful, almost vulnerable captions about literature. Now, there it was, a direct line. A small, almost imperceptible tremor ran through my hand as I read his reply: "Good to see you engaging with the material, Alex." The air in my small dorm room suddenly felt thick, heavy, like wading through deep water. I quickly minimized the window, my gaze darting to the closed door, suddenly hyper-aware of every creak in the floorboards. Every subsequent message felt like an extension of his presence, an inescapable tether pulling me deeper into his orbit. I stood up, pacing the narrow space between my desk and the window, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest.

[story 2]

I stared at my phone, the screen illuminating the pale sheen of sweat on my forehead. The profile picture—Mr. Harrison, smiling faintly in front of a misty mountain—was mocking me. It wasn't just the knowing glance in his recent comment about my essay; it was the persistent, digital footprint of his attention. I tried to focus on my textbook, the dense paragraphs blurring into meaningless shapes. But every few minutes, my eyes flicked to the corner of my screen, anticipating the next chime. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my hands tremble so badly I had to press them flat against the desk. When he sent another message, a simple question about a concept we’d discussed last week, my response came out clipped, strained. "Yes, sir. I understand." The words tasted like ash. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning beneath me, and slowly, deliberately, I pulled my headphones over my ears, trying to build a wall of sound between myself and the relentless, quiet surveillance.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the library seemed too bright, exposing every anxious flicker in my eyes. I was supposed to be studying macroeconomics, but my attention was fractured, constantly pulled toward the small icon in the corner of my feed. It was him. He’d liked a post I’d made about a historical figure, and now the direct messages were flowing, an endless stream of polite, yet undeniably personal, interaction. A strange, suffocating pressure built behind my ribs, making it hard to draw a full breath. I found myself compulsively refreshing the screen, a nervous habit I couldn't break, my fingers tapping a frantic rhythm on the keyboard. When he sent a follow-up, a casual observation about my recent performance in class, I felt a sudden, sharp urge to flee. I shoved my books haphazardly into my bag, the sound of the papers rustling sounding deafeningly loud in the cavernous space. I needed distance, desperately needing to disappear from this visible, inescapable connection.
